coredata.dc:title: Thermophysical and rheological properties of water-based graphene quantum dots nanofluids
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Amiri, Ahmad
Shanbedi, Mehdi
Dashti, Hossein
coredata.dc:description: Application of the suspended solid particles in the base fluids is one of the novel methods to increase the thermal performance of heat transfer fluids. However, the major problems with this technique are the short time colloidal stability, low thermal conductivity of additive as well as significant negative effects on rheological properties once loading nanoparticles. The present study explores, for the first time, the impacts of graphene quantum dot (GQD) on the colloidal stability and thermophysical properties of water-based GQD suspensions as a new generation of heat transfer fluid. To this end, amine-treated GQD (A-GQD) were synthesized with a novel method. Surface functionality groups on A-GQD were analyzed by XPS. Atomic-force microscopy (AFM), UV–vis spectrometry, zeta potential and average particle size techniques have been used in order to measure and evaluate the colloidal stability, size and thickness of A-GQD. After applying A-GQD as an additive, colloidal stability results indicate no sedimentation after a 30-day period. In addition, all the thermophysical properties e.g. thermal conductivity, density and viscosity were measured experimentally. The viscosity of the water-based A-GQD samples was tested at various shear rates, concentrations and temperatures. Further, it has shown that by loading A-GODs in the water, the increasing rate of the density and viscosity is not significant. Interestingly, the water-based A-GQD nanofluids at very low concentration significantly increase the thermal conductivity in comparison with that of pure water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nanofluid
Stability
Viscosity
Density
Thermal conductivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The C1s and N1s XPS spectra of A-GQDs.
AFM ichnography, 3D images and cross-section contour of A-GQD.
(a) UV/vis absorption spectrum of water-based A-GQD nanofluids. (b) Relative concentration versus time to show the colloidal stability of water-based A-GQD nanofluids.
Zeta potential of water-based A-GQD suspension at 0.001 wt.% after (a) 1 day, (b) 15 days, (c) 30 days, and water-based A-GQD suspension at 0.002 wt.% after (d) 1 day, (e) 15 days, (f) 30 days.
The effect of A-GQD concentration on the zeta potential.
Particle size distribution intensity of A-GQDs.

(a) Experimental results on the measured thermal conductivity values for water and water-based A-GQDs nanofluids against temperature at different A-GQDs weight concentrations, (b) the thermal conductivity ratio of K nf/K bf at various weight concentrations of A-GQDs.
Viscosity versus shear rate at different temperatures for water-based A-GQDs nanofluids and Di-water.
Viscosity versus temperature for water-based A-GQDs nanofluids and Di-water.
Summary of experimental results on thermal conductivity of carbon allotropes-based nanofluids.
Densities of water-based A-GQDs nanofluids and Di-water as functions of temperature and weight concentration of A-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Thermophysical and rheological properties of water-based graphene quantum dots nanofluids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Malaya, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, School of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering, Curtin University, WA, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, School of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Application of the suspended solid particles in the base fluids is one of the novel methods to increase the thermal performance of heat transfer fluids. However, the major problems with this technique are the short time colloidal stability, low thermal conductivity of additive as well as significant negative effects on rheological properties once loading nanoparticles. The present study explores, for the first time, the impacts of graphene quantum dot (GQD) on the colloidal stability and thermophysical properties of water-based GQD suspensions as a new generation of heat transfer fluid. To this end, amine-treated GQD (A-GQD) were synthesized with a novel method. Surface functionality groups on A-GQD were analyzed by XPS. Atomic-force microscopy (AFM), UV–vis spectrometry, zeta potential and average particle size techniques have been used in order to measure and evaluate the colloidal stability, size and thickness of A-GQD. After applying A-GQD as an additive, colloidal stability results indicate no sedimentation after a 30-day period. In addition, all the thermophysical properties e.g. thermal conductivity, density and viscosity were measured experimentally. The viscosity of the water-based A-GQD samples was tested at various shear rates, concentrations and temperatures. Further, it has shown that by loading A-GODs in the water, the increasing rate of the density and viscosity is not significant. Interestingly, the water-based A-GQD nanofluids at very low concentration significantly increase the thermal conductivity in comparison with that of pure water.

ce:section.ce:para: The poor thermal conductivity of conventional heat transfer fluids such as air, deionized (DI) water, ethylene glycol (EG) and engine oil is one of the biggest problems in high heat transfer application in mechanical equipment and engineering processes. The addition of ultrafine solids particles suspended in the base fluid could enhance the thermal conductivity of fluids [1–5]. The early studies indicated that the low dispersion stability of suspended particles with sizes in the range of millimeters or micrometers could have an adverse influence on the effective thermal conductivity. Their poor stability suspension leads to abrasion and channel clogging [6]. However, recently it was found that nanosized particles (1–100nm) suspensions in a common fluid can result in more stable and high thermal conductivity as well as improved rheological properties [7,8]. The term of ‘‘nanofluid’’ was first used by Choi in 1995 [9]. Several studies have shown an increased interest in nanofluids in order to improve heat transfer, stability and thermophysical properties performance [10,11].

Several types of additives have been identified in the research community: metals, metal oxides e.g. titanium oxide (TiO2), aluminum oxide (Al2O3) and carbon-based nanostructures e.g. carbon nanotubes, graphene. The main problem of metal- and metal oxide-based nanoparticles is their sedimentation in the common base fluid. Carbon-based nanostructures are capable to be functionalized covalently and non-covalently to change from a hydrophobic structure to hydrophilic one. Recently, researchers have shown an increased interest in development of different carbon-based nanostructures to prepare nanofluids like single-walled carbon nanotubes, double-walled carbon nanotubes, multi-walled carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene nanoplatelets and graphene oxide [12–16]. Graphene as a single atomic plane of graphite has high electrical and thermal conductivity, high specific surface area (SSA), high fracture strength and highly ordered graphitic carbon those attract lots of attention for the application in processes with high heat flux such as coolant technology [17]. The thermal conductivity of graphene has been measured to be in the range of 2000–5350W/m K at room temperature [18–23]. The trivial interaction between graphene and other materials, presence of micro-sized dimensions, restacking and agglomeration in different base fluids are the main concerns about the application of graphene as an additive [14]. Owning a micro-sized dimension can be considered as a fundamental limitation for the Brownian motion of graphene in base fluids [22].

Several researchers found that based on the micro-convection model, the Brownian motion of the nanoparticles in the base fluid induced local convection [24–26]. Shima et al. (2009) showed that Reynolds number in Brownian motion has the inverse proportional with the square root of the particle diameter [26]. Consequently, as the particle size decreases the thermal conductivity will increase significantly. So, aggregation and restacking can intensify this problem. To have a carbon-based additive with completely nano-sized dimensions, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be applied. GQDs have been produced from various carbon-based materials such as graphene oxides [27–31], carbon fibers [32] and carbon nanotubes [33,34].

This study provides new insights into the application of GQDs to improve the thermal conductivity of nanofluids. To this end, Amine-treated GQDs (A-GQDs) as fully nano-sized dimensions with superior thermal conductivity and capability of functionalization have been applied as additives for the preparation of stable nanofluids. A-GQDs are morphologically characterized to study the size of particles. Finally, the colloidal stability and thermophysical properties of the water-based A-GQD nanofluids are comprehensively ascertained.

In this study, a modified Hummers method was used to produce the graphite oxide sheets from natural graphite powder [35]. The synthesis procedure for GQDs was done by adapting the procedure employed by Zhang et al. (2013) with slight modification [35]:
(1)
2.0 g of the graphite were oxidized under mild ultrasonication in the concentrated HNO3 (60mL, 38wt.%) and H2SO4 (180mL, 28–30 wt.%) for 48 h.
(2)
After enough cooling, the mixture was then diluted with 1200mL of deionized water and centrifuged at 4000rpm to separate unexfoliated material.
(3)
The supernatant was diluted with the deionized water (2000mL) and sonicated for 30 min at room temperature.
(4)
The colloidal suspension was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (Mw cut-off: 3500Da) overnight. In this step, it is necessary to remove the acidic groups decorated on the edge of GQDs.
(5)
Typically, 100mL of colloidal solution was mixed with deionized water (100mL) and ammonia solution (120mL, 28–30%) in a vessel. The black suspension was sonicated in a 300W probe sonicator for 30min. Then the mixture was stirred for 4h at 100 °C. After sufficient cooling to achieve the room temperature, the black suspension centrifuged at 25,000rpm and the supernatant was collected. In the reaction step, most oxidized GQDs changed to the amine-treated GQDs (A-GQDs).
(6)
A rotary evaporator was used to concentrate the A-GQDs solution at 50 °C. Then by drying the solution in a vacuum oven, the dried A-GQDs powder was obtained. A-GQDs were properly dispersed in Distilled water using an ultrasonication probe (Sonics Vibra-Cell, VC 750, Sonics & Materials, Inc., USA) with a power supply of (20kHz) frequency and output power of (750W). It is noteworthy that synthesized GQDs are soluble in aqueous solution [36].

The Characterization of the A-GQD dispersions was investigated using atomic force microscopy (AFM, ScanAsyst mode, frequency 1Hz, Bruker). The thermal conductivity has been determined by a KD2 Pro thermal analyzer (Decagon Devices, USA). The thermal analyzer works according to transient of hot wire technique, and it has the accuracy of about 4%. The rheological behavior of water-based A-GQD nanofluids was determined using a shear-rate controlled Anton Paar rotational rheometer (model Physica MCR301, Anton Paar GmbH) based on the double gap DG 26.7 measuring system. The density of synthesized nanofluids as well as water was measured by Mettler Toledo DE-40 density meter. The accuracy of this device is ±10−4 g/cm3. All the density measurements performed in triplicate for each temperature.

In this study, UV–vis spectroscopy is also performed using Shimadzu UV-spectrometer (A Shimadzu UV-2600, Kyoto, Japan), which operates in the range of wavelengths 200–700nm. The light absorbance of all the samples was determined at specific time intervals for more than 15 days using special quartz cuvettes suitable for UV region. In order to validate the accuracy and reproducibility of the UV–vis spectrophotometer results, each absorbance reading was repeated twice. The maximum difference in the results was about 0.05%. The Zeta potential tests on the synthesized nanofluids were performed using Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, UK) by the Electrophoretic Light Scattering (ELS) technique. The zeta potential results indicate that the degree of repulsion between adjacent particles is of the same charge in the dispersion.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze A-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , the C1s XPS spectrum of A-GQDs reveals four components corresponding to different functional groups: C–C (284.6eV) of the sp2 carbons in the basal plane of the GQDs, C–O (286.5eV), CO (287.2eV), and O–CO (289.0eV). Also, there is a component at 285.5eV, which is attributed to the C–N bond in the C1s XPS spectrum of A-GQDs. The amidation reaction for preparing A-GQDs structures is strongly supported by the N1s XPS analysis. The N1s peak at 399.3eV is attributed to the nitrogen in C(O)N, while the nitrogen in –NH2 is observed as the peak at 400.6eV [37]. It can be seen that the nitrogen content of the N–CO bond in A-GQDs is ∼51%. It shows that the carboxylic groups decorated on GQDs have covalently attached to the amine groups for forming A-GQDs. From C1s spectrum, it can be seen that the extent of carboxyl O–CO band is significantly lower than C–N band, indicating covalently-functionalization of GQDs with –NH2. Further, from N1s spectrum, the same proportion of nitrogen in the N–CO and –NH2 bonds indicates that there was not any unreacted ammonia in the final sample and the majority of the amine molecules have attached covalently to GQDs.

The morphological study of A-GQD to measure the properties including size and thickness has been done using atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. 2 ). To control the size of A-GQDs, a dialysis process has been used. The diameter of A-GQD particles is in the range of 5–20nm. The results indicate the average height of particles is less than 1nm, and it is similar to chemically treated graphene nanoribbons with one layer or the monolayer of functionalized graphene quantum dots [31,38]. The results of cross-section contour further support the idea that the all the A-GQDs are single-layered. Furthermore, the results obtained from the 3D AFM images confirmed the production of uniform A-GQDs.

The results of the absorption spectra of the water-based A-GQD sample are shown in Fig. 3 a. It indicates that the water-based A-GQD sample is able to absorb visible light in the wavelength range of 200–400nm. Furthermore, the sharp absorption band at ca. 236nm is likely to be related to the π→π* transition of CC bond [19,31]. From the Fig. 3a, it can be seen that another peak at ca. 320nm is due to the presence of n→π* transition of the CO [39].

Also, it defined that the relative concentration is the ratio of subsequent weight concentration of the sample to that of the fresh one. The calculated relative concentration of all samples is presented in Fig. 3b. It is striking from the results that A-GQDs in water have a stable condition under UV–vis irradiation. By way of illustration, the degradation rate of A-GQD in water is observed to be less than 0.5% after one month. This case has shown that there is an effective colloidal stability of A-GQD in aqueous media under visible light irradiation. The most obvious finding to emerge from the analysis is that A-GQD shows no sedimentation in water. These results further can be compared with the other nanoparticles and carbon nanostructures [40]. Other possible explanations for the superior dispersion of the A-GQDs are the easily-miscible amine functionalities on the edge and large surface-to-weight ratio.

A particle size and zeta potential analyzer was used to measure surface charges and detect the potential effects of the dispersion medium for all particulate samples. Zeta potential can be used to identify the stability of a colloidal suspension, and there is a direct relationship between them [41]. This relationship can be explained by the fact that the mutual repulsion that occurs between same charged particles. Therefore, due to the repulsive forces, the particles with a high surface charge do not get to agglomerate [42,43]. Consequently, a good colloidal stability for a nanofluid suspension is defined when the measured zeta potential is above absolute value of 30mV [43,44]. Fig. 4 provides the measured zeta potential values for the water-based A-GQDs nanofluids. All the zeta potential tests were performed in triplicate. The deviations between curves in three separate tests were insignificant, indicating superior homogeneity of A-GQDs in base fluid. It is also apparent from this figure results that there is no significant sedimentation after 30 days. Further, it showed a good agreement between the zeta results and the trend of data obtained with the UV–vis tests. Again, the absolute value of zeta potential is a measure of dispersion stability. For water-based A-GQDs nanofluid at 0.002 wt.%, absolute zeta potentials of ∼50mV is a strong evidence on the long-term stability of samples. The small change in zeta potential is sufficient to ensure colloidal stability of dispersions for long period of time. Note that there are several illustrations in literature where stable dispersions of nanoparticles have been prepared in water through the adjustment of pH [45], which mostly makes the pH of nanofluids acidic or basic. Interestingly, decoration of GQDs with amine groups could moderate acidity, resulting in having natural nanofluids.

Fig. 5 shows the effect of A-GQD concentration on the zeta-potential values. It can be seen that there is a concentration range ((0.002 to 0.02) wt.%) in which the zeta-potential values were consistent (49.8 to 52.1mV), but on lower concentration, the zeta-potential changed dramatically, to 26.4 for 0.001 wt.%. From Fig. 5, there is a distinctive region in the plots of zeta-potential versus particle concentration, in which the zeta-potential value is independent of nanoparticle concentration, which can be labeled as “stable region”. On the other hand, the value of the zeta-potential decreases dramatically as the concentration decreases at weight concentration less than 0.002, which can be labeled as “unstable region”.

There are two possible scenarios that can explain the existence of the unstable region, depending upon whether the effect is considered to be either real or an artifact. If it is a real effect, then the shift in the zeta-potential values can be attributed to an unstable suspension, which subsequently results in the aggregation of nanoparticles. This interpretation is similar to that given by [46], in which the author observed a similar effect when studying the dilution of an emulsion of micron size droplets of hexadecane in water. According to Medrzycka [46], the source of the instability was explained by changes in the extent of ions being absorbed on the particles that occurred within a designated concentration range. In our case, however, it is very unlikely that this interpretation can be extended to offer a viable explanation to the observed results.

Regarding artifact effect, according to Ref. [47], as the nanoparticle concentration decreases, the signal arising from any extraneous particles in the media will become more dominant. Hence, what is measured, arising from a combination of both nanoparticles and extraneous particles, will become more dependent on the extraneous particles. The same results were reported by Tantra et al. [47] for different dispersions. They employed different nanoparticles e.g. hollow-shaped MWCNT functionalized with carboxyl groups, bamboo-shaped MWCNT functionalized with carboxyl groups, gold nanoparticles, and Ludox (silica). As one of the main results, they concluded that the mean value of the zeta-potential changed dramatically at very low weight concentrations, which referred to this concentration range as the “unstable region”. Also, it can be concluded from their results that the range of the unstable region is different for different additives.

Further analysis illustrated the determined average particle size for the water-based A-GQDs nanofluids. It can be seen that the mean particle sizes for all samples at various concentrations were almost 17nm (Fig. 6 ). The most striking observation to emerge from the results was that the average particle size did not change after a period of 30-day, which reveals an excellent stability of the colloidal suspensions samples.

Thermal conductivity measurements have been performed by the KD2 Pro thermal properties analyzer. This analyzer used the transient hot-wire approach, and it has the designed accuracy of about 2.458%. It is now well established from a variety of studies that the thermal conductivity of nanofluids depends on several factors including temperature, thermal conductivities of the suspended nanoparticles and the base fluid, surface area, volume fraction, interfacial thermal resistance and shape or geometry of nanoparticles [14,48].

Fig. 7 (a) presents the experimental results on the measured thermal conductivity values for water and water-based A-GQDs nanofluids against temperature at various A-GQDs weight concentrations. It can be seen from the results that an increase in temperature and weight concentration of A-GQDs leads to an increase in the value of thermal conductivity for the water-based A-GQDs nanofluids. In this paper, the term of thermal conductivity ratio is used to refer to the ratio of the measured thermal conductivity of nanofluid to that of the base fluid (K nf/K bf). The following definition is intended to present the enhancement in thermal conductivity when the A-GQDs are loaded in water. The values for thermal conductivity ratio at different weight concentrations of A-GQDs are shown in Fig. 7b. As shown in Fig. 7(a), by increasing the temperature from 20 to 50 °C, the thermal conductivity for the water-based A-GQDs nanofluids increased from 0.632 to 0.701W/m K at the concentration of 0.001 wt.%; and from 0.701 to 0.758W/m K at 0.002 wt.% concentration. Similarly, as can be seen from Fig. 7b, the maximum increase in the thermal conductivity ratio of ca. 10.1% at 0.001 wt.% and 18.6% at 0.002 wt.% have been observed.

There are several possible explanations for the unexpected growth of the thermal conductivity in nanofluids. Four suggested controlling mechanisms have been proposed in the literature [49,50]; 1) Brownian motion of the nanoparticles, 2) molecular-level layering of the liquid at the liquid/particle interface, 3) the effects of nanoparticle clustering and 4) the different nature of heat transport in the nanoparticles. In a study by Wang et al. (1999), they found that there is a relationship between the thermal conductivity of nanofluids along with the particle structure and the microscopic motion [51]. Microscopic motion is including Brownian motion and inter-particle forces. In another research by Koo and Kleinstreuer (2005), they concluded that nanoparticle Brownian motion is playing like a much more vital parameter in comparison with the thermo- and osmo-phoretic motion effects [52]. While the Brownian motion effect depended on the particle size, the hermo-phoretic and osmo-phoretic motion effects were observed to be independent of the size of the particle. Taken together, the studies presented thus far provide evidence that any substantial enhancement in thermal conductivity of A-GQD-based samples is mainly related to the higher values of local convection, affected by the higher Brownian motion of A-GQD in base fluid comparing with Brownian motion effects of other carbon nanostructures those have micro-dimensions.

To have a comparison between other nanofluids including graphene, Table 1 summarizes the results of thermal conductivity measurement of different researchers, where carbon allotropes-based nanofluids were employed.

The results of measured densities of water-based A-GQDs nanofluids and Di-water as functions of temperature and weight concentration of A-GQDs are presented in Table 2 . It is apparent from this table that by increasing the temperature the densities of water-based A-GQDs nanofluids and Di-water decrease. This result could be attributed to the thermal expansion of the liquid. Furthermore, Table 2 reveals that there has been a rise in the density of samples by increasing the weight concentration of A-GQDs. It can be explained by the fact that the solid particles have a higher density than the liquid and it can result in increasing in density by increasing the weight concentration of A-GQDs. The results illustrate that there has been a slight rise in density during the increases in the temperature. From the results in Table 2, the amounts of increase in density after loading A-GQD in water were less than 0.1% for both samples at different temperatures, which is considered as a great potential.

The results of viscosity versus shear rate (20–300 s−1) at different temperatures (20, 30, 40, and 50 °C) of water-based A-GQDs nanofluids and Di-water are presented in Fig. 8 . In this figure, the measurement of the viscosity of Di-water, as well as the standard values of viscosity of Di-water in order to validate the reliability of viscosity results from the rheometer, have been illustrated. The standard values of viscosity of Di-water have been extracted from literature data [75]. The average values of the measured viscosity at different temperatures have been calculated and the comparison with the standard value results in a maximum error of 3%. Therefore, it is confirmed that the rheometer is reliable for viscosity measurement of nanofluid with an acceptable accuracy. As can be seen from Fig. 8, the current study found that water-based A-GQDs nanofluids at weight concentrations of 0.001 and 0.002 are categorized as the Newtonian fluids in all ranges of shear rate. Furthermore, the average values of viscosity are higher than those of Di-water with (1.00 and 2.11% at 20 °C), (1.10, and 2.20% at 30 °C), (1.84, and 3.54% at 40 °C), and (1.54, and 3.58% at 50 °C).

It seems possible that Newtonian behavior of the water-based A-GQDs nanofluids is due to the low concentration of A-GQDs as well as the nano-sized dimension of A-GQDs. Consequently, it can be inferred that the A-GQDs loading effects on the viscosity of base fluid are much lower than the other nanofluids [75–78]. This finding has important implications for developing A-GQDs as additives in fluid flow which could lead to decrease the pressure loss as well as pumping power in industrial applications.

Also, the average viscosities of water and water-based A-GQD nanofluids at different concentrations as a function of temperature are shown in Fig. 9 . Again, there is insignificant difference between synthesized nanofluids and Di-water.

Among several methods to increase the thermal performance of heat transfer fluids, using the suspended solid particles in the base fluids has sparked huge interest in a wide range of scientific and industrial processes. This paper set out to investigate the impact of loading A-GQDs into the water at different A-GQDs’ concentrations on the stability, thermophysical and rheological properties of water-based A-GQDs nanofluids. A-GQDs were synthesized and used in this study by a relatively quick method. The following conclusions can be drawn from the present study:
•
There is an acceptable level of stability of the dispersed A-GQDs in aqueous media.
•
The results of stability analysis indicated that there is not any sedimentation in the water-based A-GQDs nanofluids after a period of the 30 days. Further, the UV–vis spectrometry experiments illustrated the highest dispersion stability of 0.992-relative concentration for the 0.001-wt.% A-GQDs at the same period.
•
These experiments reveal the Newtonian behavior of water-based A-GQDs nanofluids at different temperatures and the weight concentrations of A-GQDs.
•
As the temperature and the weight concentration of A-GQDs increase the measured value for thermal conductivity increases.
•
Based on the emerging results of the thermal conductivity, colloidal stability and viscosity analyses, these findings have significant implications of water-based A-GQDs nanofluids as promising working fluids in different heat transfer industrial applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Vira Carbon Nano Materials (VCN Materials Co.) for providing raw materials and facilities for this study.
